definition: The probability of financial loss resulting from a borrower's failure to repay a loan or meet contractual obligations, a key driver of Non-Performing Loans (NPL).
The compression of the spread between interest income earned on assets (like loans) and interest paid on liabilities (like deposits), usually driven by interest rate movements.
A standard banking regulation requirement ensuring banks maintain an adequate level of high-quality liquid assets that can be converted into cash to meet 30-day stress scenarios.
A loan that is outstanding and on which no interest or principal payments have been made for a specified period (typically 90 days or more).
A metric assessing bank liquidity and leverage by dividing total outstanding loans by total deposits. Extremely high LDR indicates higher liquidity risk.
